content.problemStatement:
Once upon a time chef decided to learn encodings. And, obviously, he started with the easiest one (well, actually the easiest after Caesar cypher) – substitution cypher.

 
But very soon Chef got bored with encoding/decoding, so he started thinking how to hack this cypher. 
He already knows some algorithm, which is not always correct, 
but it’s sufficient for now. Here is its description.




Imagine we know frequency sequence of English letters (this means, that letters are sorted by their frequency of appearing in English texts, in ascending order).
And let’s find frequency sequence of cyphered letters (if some of them appear equal number of times, then first in frequency sequence will be lower letter between them).
Now, using this two frequency sequences we can recover plain text. Just substitute cyphered letter with origin one, if they are at same positions in sequences. 



Now, Chef has frequency sequence of English letters and cypher text. And he asks you to recover plain text. Please, help him.
constraints: The frequency sequence length is exactly 26 and is a permutation of 'a'-'z'.
Sum of lengths of all encrypted texts ≤ 150000
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line is a string of length 26 containing all lowercase English letters – the frequency sequence of English letters (ordered from least frequent to most frequent). The second line is the encrypted text, which may contain any printable characters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line – the decrypted text obtained by the described frequency-based substitution. The case of alphabetic characters must be preserved, all non-alphabetic characters are printed unchanged.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: frequency of letters in "dummy!" gives order a,b,c,...,d,u,y,m. Mapping according to the given English sequence yields "hello!". Second test case similarly produces "have a nice day!". Third test case preserves the capital D, resulting in "Hello!".
The frequency sequence is the standard English alphabet, so the mapping is the identity function, and the decrypted text is the same as the encrypted text.

The frequency sequence is the reverse of the standard English alphabet, so the mapping is the reverse function, and the decrypted text is the encrypted text with each letter replaced by its corresponding letter from the end of the alphabet.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by first analyzing the frequency of characters in the given encrypted text and then mapping them to the corresponding characters in the frequency sequence of English letters. This mapping is used to substitute the characters in the encrypted text to obtain the decrypted text.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N log N) per test case, where N is the length of the encrypted text. The overall time complexity is O(T * N log N) due to the sorting operation.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the array used to store the frequency of characters in the encrypted text.
